title: Chapter 38: Fortification
content: The week passed quickly.
Roxanne spent it buying.
Not clothes. Not coffee. Defenses.
She bought a barrier from the Crossroads — a stabilized reality field that would prevent unauthorized teleportation into the Vance Demesne. Cost: fifty thousand Infinity Notes.

She bought a warning system from a Deep Paths oracle — a network of sensory nodes that could detect dimensional rifts before they opened. Cost: twenty thousand Infinity Notes, plus a promise of future trade exclusivity.

She bought weapons. Not for herself. For the Executioner. The silent entity had been her guard since the first day, and it deserved an upgrade. She found a dealer in the Deep Paths who sold crystallized void energy — ammunition that could harm even 11th Layer entities. Cost: seventy thousand Infinity Notes.
The Executioner received the upgrade in silence. It simply nodded once and attached the new ammunition to its chainsaw.
Lena watched from the quad, sketching. "You're building an army."
"I'm building a wall. There's a difference."
"Does the wall include the refugees?"
Roxanne paused.
"What do you mean?"
"The 11th Layer said they'd come for you. But they'll have to go through everyone else first. Are you going to evacuate them?"
Roxanne had not considered evacuation.
"Where would they go?"
"I don't know. But staying here when a cosmic horror shows up doesn't seem like a great option."
She looked at the campus. Seven thousand people. Families. Children. Old people who had survived the initial apocalypse only to find themselves in the middle of a war between gods and economists.
"I can't evacuate seven thousand people in a week."
"Then give them a way to survive."
"How?"
Lena held up her sketchbook.
"Teach them. Give them jobs. Make them part of the defense. If they're just waiting to be saved, they'll panic. But if they have something to do, they'll hold."
Roxanne looked at her friend.
"When did you get so smart about warfare?"
"I watched you for six months. You buy things. I draw things. We both learn."
Roxanne smiled.
"Okay. Let's build a real defense."
The next day, Roxanne turned the campus into a fortress.

Not a military fortress. A civilian defense network. Every able-bodied adult was assigned a role: lookout, messenger, supply runner, shelter coordinator. The medical clinic trained volunteers in triage. The dining hall stockpiled rations. The children were taught where to go if the sky tore open.
Frost calculated optimal evacuation routes. Silas reinforced the barrier at key points. The Executioner patrolled the perimeter, its upgraded chainsaw crackling with void energy.
And Kallos arrived from the Crossroads with reinforcements.
"The Crossroads is with you," Kallos said, its four arms carrying crates of supplies. "If the 11th Layer attacks one independent territory, they attack all of us. We stand together."
"How many fighters?"
"Not fighters. Traders. But we know the Deep Paths better than any 11th Layer entity. If you need to move people, we can move them."
"I might need that."
"When?"
"When the barrier fails."
"You think it will fail?"
Roxanne looked at the barrier — a shimmer of stabilized reality that surrounded the campus.
"The 11th Layer has existed since before the Nether-Grid. They've seen every defense ever invented. This barrier won't stop them. It'll slow them down."
"How long?"
"Long enough for me to do what I need to do."
"Which is?"
She held up the black card.
"Buy the one thing they can't afford to lose."
That night, Roxanne sat alone in her office.
The card lay on the desk in front of her. It pulsed steadily. ∞.
She had been using it for six months. Buying territories, entities, currencies, defenses. But she had never asked the most important question:
Where did the card's value come from?

Every currency in the Nether-Grid was backed by something. Obols were backed by debt enforcement. Infinity Notes were backed by her territory's productivity. But the card — the original contract — was backed by something that predated all of that.
What?
She picked up the card. Turned it over. The back was smooth. Featureless. She had never noticed before.
But now, in the dim light of her office, she saw something.
An inscription. Faint. Almost invisible.
She held it closer.
The inscription read: *BALANCE: ∞. REDEEMABLE AT: THE PRIMORDIAL VOID.*
Roxanne stared at it.
Redeemable at the Primordial Void.
The Void that had collapsed. The Void that was supposed to be dead.
If the card was redeemable there, then the Void wasn't dead. It was just... waiting.
And she had the key.
